preamble.constraint_ref: Pattern matches are candidates, not findings. Every match below must pass through the investigation gates and false-positive checks in its play file before being flagged. If any gate produces ambiguity, exclude the candidate. It is always better to miss a real anti-pattern than to flag correct code.
preamble.instructions:
Read this file as the first step of every audit. For each play, use the description to understand the violation, then use grep helpers as starting points for search. For patterns that span multiple lines, search for the entry-point line and then read surrounding context to confirm.
After finding a match, read the play file for rules, false-positive heuristics, and false_positive exclusions in the severity_assessment block before flagging.
preamble.output_format: Report findings under "### Play NN: Play Name - SEVERITY" headings. Each finding gets "#### N. file:line - CONFIDENCE" with a code snippet. See agent_constraints in sre.agent.md.

confidence_levels.level: Flag if confirmed after reading context (outside test files)
Read 10-20 lines of surrounding context; check play file for false positives

preamble.general_exclusions: Skip spec/, test/, and fixtures/ directories unless a play specifically notes otherwise.

play.title: Don't Leak PII/PHI/Secrets
play.description: Exception messages or log statements that include raw response bodies, user params, or data that could contain PII/PHI (names, SSNs, medical info) or secrets (API keys, tokens). Common pattern: raise or logger.error with .body or raw params interpolated into the message or metadata.

pattern.context: raw upstream response body passed into rendered JSON or hash literal; check if this reaches render json: or is included in error response meta
error.try(:original_body) or error.try(:message) in hash values rendered to client; read enclosing method to see if hash is passed to render json:
render json: with error hash that may include raw upstream data; read the hash construction to check for .body, .original_body, or .message fields
vamf_body often contains raw upstream response; check if it flows into exception response_values that reach the client

play.context_check:
The original grep helpers catch the most common pattern (string interpolation of .body in raise/logger). The additional patterns above catch a second, subtler leak vector: raw upstream response data passed as hash values into error responses rendered to clients.
When you find a match on `original_body`, `vamf_body`, or `render.*json:.*error`: 1. Read the full method to identify the hash being constructed. 2. Trace each value: does it include raw upstream response data (e.g., `error.original_body`, `error.try(:original_body)`, `@env.body`, `response.body`)? 3. Determine if the hash is rendered to the client (`render json:`), included in an exception's `response_values` (which may reach ExceptionHandling and render), or only written to internal logs. 4. If rendered to clients: HIGH. If only in logs: MEDIUM. If sanitized before use (e.g., passed through an anonymizer or allowlisted to safe keys): exclude.

play.title: Preserve Cause Chains
play.description:
Rescue blocks that catch an exception and raise a new one in a way that destroys the cause chain. The main anti-pattern is converting an exception to a string with raise "error: #{e}" which creates a RuntimeError and loses the original class, backtrace, and cause chain. Ruby automatically preserves the cause chain when you raise a new exception inside a rescue block ($!.cause is set implicitly), so the fix is usually raise TypedError.new(detail) or bare raise. Do NOT recommend cause: e with Common::Exceptions classes (they don't accept it).
Note: raise e inside rescue => e DOES preserve the cause chain (Ruby sets cause implicitly). Only flag when a different exception class is raised without cause:.

pattern.context: read context to check if inside a rescue block
check if inside a rescue block (if so, cause chain is broken because the original exception is discarded)
read context to confirm rescue block

play.title: Never Use Bare Rescues
play.description: Rescue blocks without an exception class (rescue, rescue => e, or rescue Exception). These catch everything including NoMethodError from typos and SignalException from Ctrl+C. Also look for rescue blocks that return nil, false, or empty collections, which signals error swallowing combined with bare rescue.
play.context_check: Search for rescue then read surrounding lines. Rescue block followed by nil, false, [], or {} return: MEDIUM.

play.title: Map Upstream Network Errors
play.description: Rescue blocks that catch Faraday errors (timeouts, connection failures, upstream 5xx) and map them to InternalServerError (500). Upstream failures should map to 502/503/504, not 500. A 500 means our code is broken. Also look for catching all Faraday errors with a single rescue clause and returning one status code.

pattern.context: blanket catch may be intentional in service layer; check if controller or service code before flagging

play.context_check: Search for rescue\s+Faraday:: then read surrounding lines. Faraday rescue followed by InternalServerError raise: HIGH.

play.title: Match Status Codes to Fault Ownership (4xx vs 5xx)
play.description: Broad rescue blocks (bare rescue or rescue => e) that return or raise 422 UnprocessableEntity. This disguises server errors and upstream failures as client validation errors, hiding real problems from dashboards. Only specific validation exceptions should produce 422. Also check error-status mapper methods that default to 422 in an else branch: when unknown or nil error codes fall through to 422, unexpected server faults are misclassified as client input errors.

pattern.context: search for usages, then read the rescue clause above each one
search for the symbol form used in render status: and case/when branches; read the enclosing rescue or error-handling method to check if broad rescues route through it

catch numeric 422 in else branches of error-status mapper methods at the service layer (e.g., map_error_to_status_code); read the full case/when to check if the else branch can receive nil or unknown values
catch error-to-status mapper methods with case/when that may default unknown errors to 422; read the method and trace callers to determine what values can reach the else branch

play.context_check: Search for UnprocessableEntity, :unprocessable_entity, and `else 422` then read the enclosing rescue block or error mapper: - Bare or broad rescue raising/rendering 422: HIGH - rescue => e followed by 422: HIGH - Error-status mapper with else defaulting to 422 when the mapper is called from a broad rescue: MEDIUM (check what values can reach the else branch) - Service-layer mapper (e.g., map_error_to_status_code) with `else 422`: MEDIUM — check if this mapper runs BEFORE the controller mapper, creating a double-mapping where unknown errors are silently converted to 422 at the service layer and the controller never sees the original unknown code. Trace the full path: what error strings/codes can reach the else branch? If nil or unexpected values can reach it, the else should be 500, not 422.

play.title: Handle 401 Token Ownership
play.description: Code that catches upstream 401 errors and blindly passes them through as 401 to clients. When our service account credentials fail upstream, that's our fault (500), not the user's (401). The key question: "Whose credentials failed: the user's or ours?"

pattern.context: requires credential ownership investigation (user token vs service account) before flagging

play.context_check: Search for Unauthorized then read the rescue block. Upstream 401 passed through without checking credential ownership: HIGH.

play.title: Handle 403 Permission vs Existence
play.description: Code that returns 403 Forbidden for non-existent resources (should be 404) or for expired/missing tokens (should be 401). Also look for rescue ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound that raises Forbidden. This leaks resource existence information and enables enumeration attacks.

pattern.context: check if this is really a permission issue or a missing resource

play.context_check: Search for Forbidden and RecordNotFound in proximity. RecordNotFound rescued and converted to Forbidden: HIGH.

play.title: Prefer Typed Exceptions
play.description: raise 'string message' in HTTP request paths. These become RuntimeError, fall through to the default 500 handler, and are indistinguishable from each other in APM. Should be typed exceptions that map to correct HTTP status codes.

play.title: Expected vs Unexpected Errors
play.description: Code that calls span.set_error or set_error() on 4xx exceptions. Expected client errors (400, 401, 404, 422) should not mark APM spans as errors; this floods error tracking with non-bugs. Only 5xx errors are unexpected and should be marked.

pattern.context: must check if request-level span or internal sub-span, and if guarded by status >= 500
must check if request-level span or internal sub-span, and if inside a conditional for 5xx only

play.title: Don't Build Module-Specific Frameworks
play.description: Custom logging services, error handler classes, monitoring wrappers, tracing helpers, or error classification methods built within a module. These fragment the codebase (vets-api has 24+ of these). Modules should use Rails.logger, StatsD, Datadog::Tracing, and the ExceptionHandling concern directly. Also look for handle_error or handle_exception methods embedded in service classes. These reimplement error classification that belongs in the platform layer, even without a standalone framework class.

pattern.context: check it's not a domain model
